coredata.dc:title: Deep eutectic solvents-derived carbon dots for detection of mercury (II), photocatalytic antifungal activity and fluorescent labeling for C. albicans
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Zhe
Li, Xiao
Shi, Longyan
Yang, Yaling
coredata.dc:description: A novel nitrogen and chloride co-doped carbon dots (N/Cl-CDs) based choline chloride-urea deep eutectic solvents (DESs) were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method with high quantum yield of 37% and excellent photoluminescent properties. This N/Cl-CDs fluorescent probe had been successfully applied to sensitively and selectively detect the concentration of Hg2+ with a linear range of 0.2–40 μM and a detection limit of 0.05 μM. Moreover, the N/Cl-CDs displayed a strong photocatalytic antifungal activity against C. albicans and their photoinduced antifungal functions were evaluated under conditions of varying other experimental parameters. The antifungal efficiency of N/Cl-CDs (7 mg/mL) against C. albicans is upon 100% when extending the visible light irradiation time to 80 min. In addition, the excellent luminescence properties of N/Cl-CDs can also label C. albicans and displayed multicolour fluorescence imaging at different excitation wavelengths. Based on their functions of fluorescence probe, antifungal and fluorescence imaging, N/Cl-CDs would provide potentials for a wide range of applications in the detection and microbe in the near future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
DESs
Fluorescent probe
Antifungal
Multicolour fluorescence

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Culture and enumeration of the C. albicans
Fluorescence probe of N/Cl-CDs toward Hg2+
Antifungal effect of N/Cl-CDs on C. albicans
Photocatalytic disinfection tests to C. albicans

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic illustration of the synthesis and the application of N/Cl-CDs.

(a) Fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra of N/Cl-CDs. Inset: photograph of N/Cl-CDs solution without or with UV irradiation (365 nm). (b) Fluorescent emission spectra of the N/Cl-CDs at different excitation wavelength from 300 to 390 nm. (c) TEM image of N/Cl-CDs.
XPS spectra of the N/Cl-CDs (a), high resolution C 1s (b), N 1s (c), and O 1s (d) peaks of the N/Cl-CDs.

Fluorescence intensity of the selectivity of the N/Cl-CDs for Hg2+ detection in the presence of 25 μM of various metal ions (a) and anions (b). (c) The fluorescence intensity of N/Cl-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of Hg2+. (d) Photographs of the N/Cl-CDs solution containing different concentrations of Hg2+ under daylight (left) and UV light (right, excited at 365 nm). (e) A linear correlation between the fluorescence intensity and the concentrations of Hg2+.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of N/Cl-CDs and N/Cl-CDs + Hg2+. (b) Fluorescence lifetime of N/Cl-CDs and N/Cl-CDs containing Hg2+. (c) Stern–Volmer curves for the N/Cl-CDs with the Hg2+ solution system at three different temperatures (298 K; 308 K; 318 K).

(a) Reductions in the viable cell number after C. albicans cells were treated with the N/Cl-CDs for 90 min with or without light. (b) Bacterial colony growth in the presence of N/Cl-CDs with C. albicans under different visible light illumination. (c) Bacterial colony growth in the presence of N/Cl-CDs with C. albicans under different irradiation times. (d) Bactericidal efficiency against C. albicans with under different concentrations of N/Cl-CDs.
SEM images of C. albicans treated with N/Cl-CDs under visible light irradiation for (a) 0 min, (b) 20 min, (c) 40 min and (d) 80 min.
Schematic illustration of photocatalytic inactivation of C. albicans process by N/Cl-CDs.
Fluorescent images of C. albicans labeled with N/Cl-CDs were captured under the excitation of 405, 488 and 552 nm, respectively.
Performance comparison of the proposed method with the some previously reported fluorescence sensors for the detection of Hg2+.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Average of three replicate measurements ± standard deviation.

head.ce:title: Deep eutectic solvents-derived carbon dots for detection of mercury (II), photocatalytic antifungal activity and fluorescent labeling for C. albicans

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming 650500, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Life Science and Technology
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yunnan Jianniu Bio Technology Co., Ltd, Kunming 650033, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Yunnan Jianniu Bio Technology Co., Ltd

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel nitrogen and chloride co-doped carbon dots (N/Cl-CDs) based choline chloride-urea deep eutectic solvents (DESs) were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method with high quantum yield of 37% and excellent photoluminescent properties. This N/Cl-CDs fluorescent probe had been successfully applied to sensitively and selectively detect the concentration of Hg2+ with a linear range of 0.2–40 μM and a detection limit of 0.05 μM. Moreover, the N/Cl-CDs displayed a strong photocatalytic antifungal activity against C. albicans and their photoinduced antifungal functions were evaluated under conditions of varying other experimental parameters. The antifungal efficiency of N/Cl-CDs (7 mg/mL) against C. albicans is upon 100% when extending the visible light irradiation time to 80 min. In addition, the excellent luminescence properties of N/Cl-CDs can also label C. albicans and displayed multicolour fluorescence imaging at different excitation wavelengths. Based on their functions of fluorescence probe, antifungal and fluorescence imaging, N/Cl-CDs would provide potentials for a wide range of applications in the detection and microbe in the near future.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past few years, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted widely public attention. CDs are a new type of nanomaterials with superior water dispersible, chemical inertness, excellent optical properties, good biocompatibility, high photostability, and photocatalysts [1–3]. Due to these characteristic features CDs can be used for analytical determination, disease detection, drug delivery, fluorescent ink, electrochemistry, sensor, catalysis and bioimaging [4–7]. CDs have drawn increasing attention due to their low cytotoxicity relative to metal-based quantum dots. So far, varieties of methods have been developed for CDs synthesization, such as including electrochemical [8], arc discharge [9], ultrasonic [10], acid oxidation [11], microwave digestion [12], hydrothermal [13]. Furthermore, many kinds of literatures were reported that CDs synthesized by using various plant materials and applied to detect metal ions and cell image [14–17].

Recently, deep eutectic solvents (DESs) have emerged and attracted considerable attention [18]. DESs can be formed simply by the hydrogen bonding between hydrogen bond acceptor (HBA) and hydrogen bond donor (HBD), which mixed and heated at a proper molar eutectic ratio [19,20]. In literature, DESs have the potential to be used in catalyst, extraction, separation electrochemistry and chromatography due to their significant advantages in low toxicity, thermal stability and biocompatibility [21–23]. It has been reported that CDs have been synthesize in an eco-friendly DESs [24,25]. Wang reported that DESs-assisted preparation of nitrogen/chloride-doped carbon dots for intracellular biological sensing and live cell imaging [26]. Antonello demonstrated a novel preparative route toward zwitterionic natural DESs as environmentally friendly media for spontaneous self-assembly of gold nanoparticles [27].

In the present work, the nitrogen and chloride co-doped carbon dots (N/Cl-CDs) were synthesized by choline chloride (HBA) and urea (HBD) based DESs with citric acid as the carbon source. It is remarkable that the synthesized N/Cl-CDs with high quantum yield and high stability can be used to detect mercury ions (Hg2+) in an aqueous solution. The N/Cl-CDs exhibit excellent antifungal performance against Candida albicans (C. albicans) under visible light irradiation. Moreover, the as-prepared N/Cl-CDs can be also uses as multicolour fluorescent probes to label C. albicans (Fig. 1 ).

Choline chloride (ChCl) was purchased from Macklin Biochemical Company (Shanghai, China). Citric acid and urea were purchased from J&K Scientific LTD. (Beijing, China). The metal ions (Cr3+, Cr6+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Ag+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cd2+, Al3+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Co2+) and anions (SO4 2−, SO3 2−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, I−, Cl−, Br−, IO3 −, NO2 −, PO4 3−, S2−, BrO3 −, CN−) were bought from Tianjin Fengchuan Chemical Reagent Technologies Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). C. albicans used in this study was obtained from Kunming University of Science and Technology culture collection. Sabaurauds agar (SAB) was purchased from Huankai (Guangdong, China). Phosphate buffered solution (PBS, pH 7.2–7.4) used as buffer solution was purchased from Solarbio (Beijing, China).

DESs were synthesized by mixing ChCl with urea at a molar ratio of 1:1. Briefly, 2.8 g of ChCl and 1.2 g of urea were stirred magnetically in a beaker at 100 °C until a homogeneous liquid appeared. 1.9 g of citric acid and 100 mL of deionized water were added to mix in a beaker with ChCl-urea DESs under stirring until no solid is observed. And then the mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave (150 mL) and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. After the autoclave cooled down to room temperature naturally, the large particles were removed by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 15 min and the supernatant was collected. And then the mixture was filtered with 0.22 μm filter membrane to eliminate the large particles and residual carbon nanoparticles. Finally, the solution was dialyzed with a 1000 Da dialysis membrane against deionized water for about 24 h to remove the small fragments. The formed N/Cl-CDs were stored at 4 °C before using.

Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were conducted on a FEI Tecnai G2 F30 transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA). The UV–visible absorption spectrum was examined by UV-2550 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was taken on a TENSOR27 FTIR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted on X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Kratos, UK). The fluorescence spectra were performed on a G9800A Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). The morphologies of C. albicans were observed by field emission scanning electron microscopy (S-4800 FE-SEM, Hitachi, Japan). The fluorescences of C. albicans were obtained by a confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM; Leica, TCS-SP5).

The quantum yield of N/Cl-CDs was measured by using a reference method at 350 nm excitation wavelength [28]. Quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution (quantum yield of 54% when excited at 350 nm) as the standard. The quantum yield of the N/Cl-CDs was determined using the following equation with quinine sulfate as the standard [29]:
Φ =
Φ R
×
I /
I R
×
A R
/ A
×
η /
η R
2
where “Φ” represents the quantum yield, “I” denotes the measured intensity of fluorescence spectra, “A” corresponds to the UV–vis absorbance intensity, “η” is the refractive index. The subscript “R” corresponds to the standard.

For Hg2+ determination, different concentrations of Hg2+ were mixed with the solution of N/Cl-CDs at room temperature. The mixed solution was incubated for 5 min. The fluorescence spectrum of solution was measured at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The different metals ions (Cr3+, Cr6+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Ag+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cd2+, Al3+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Co2+)and anions (SO4 2−, SO3 2−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, I−, Cl−, Br−, IO3 −, NO2 −, PO4 3−, S2−, BrO3 −, CN−)were further studied to determine the selectivity of N/Cl-CDs toward Hg2+, and all experiments were performed under the same condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Culture and enumeration of the C. albicans
ce:section.ce:para: C. albicans were cultured in Sabouraud Dextrose broth (SD broth) at 37 °C with constant shaking for 24 h. The cell suspensions were diluted with sterile PBS to obtain the cell count of approximately 107 colony-forming units per milliliter (CFU/mL).

The as-prepared N/Cl-CDs show excellent antifungal behavior. The antifungal activity of N/Cl-CDs against C. albicans was evaluated under visible-light irradiation. The different concentrations of N/Cl-CDs were prepared for further use. All materials were sterilized at 121 °C for 20 min to ensure sterility. All the experiments were processed under sterile conditions. In the photocatalytic experiment, 500 μL of the N/Cl-CDs solutions were mixed with 5 mL of the fungal suspension (107 CFU/mL) and then the reaction solutions were transferred to a transparent quartz test tube. Then the prepared samples were stirred for 10 s. The reaction solution samples were exposed to a visible LED light (16 W) for different time intervals (0, 20, 40, 60, 80 min). C. albicans without N/Cl-CDs were also exposed to the LED light as a control experiment. The same procedure was repeated under dark conditions. 100 μL of tested solution were spread on Sabouraud Dextrose Agar (SDA) plates and then cultured at 37 °C for 48 h before counting to determine the viable colony numbers. The antifungal activity of N/Cl-CDs was calculated using following equation [30]:
Antifungal efficiency
%
=
N control
−
N sample
/
N control
× 100 %
where Ncontrol and Nsample correspond to the numbers of colonies from the blank control samples and after treated with N/Cl-CDs.

Considering the strong tunable fluorescence property of N/Cl-CDs, the fluorescence imaging ability of N/Cl-CDs toward bacteria was explored. 1 mL of C. albicans (107 CFU/mL) were incubated with 500 μL of N/Cl-CDs, and then reacted together at 37 °C for 1 h under constant shaking in the dark. The mixture was centrifuged at 4000 rpm and washed with sterile PBS for 6 times. The fluorescence imaging was performed on a CLSM with different excitation wavelengths of 405, 488 and 555 nm laser pulses, respectively.

Fig. 2a illustrates the UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectroscopy of N/Cl-CDs. It can be seen that the characteristic absorption peaks of the N/Cl-CDs appeared at 236 and 334 nm, which is associated with the π-π* transition of CC bond of aromatic sp2 domains from the carbon core and n-π* transition of CO, COOH, and NH2 bonds [31,32]. The fluorescence emission wavelength is located at 444 nm corresponding to the excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The inset photograph exhibits that bright blue fluorescence of N/Cl-CDs aqueous solution under the excitation of 365 nm UV light. It was observed that the fluorescence emission peaks of N/Cl-CDs had not shifted at various excitation wavelengths, indicating the uniformity of size and the stability of surface states. As shown in Fig. 2b that the maximum emission peak at 444 nm when the maximum excitation at 350 nm.

The TEM image (Fig. 2c) revealed that N/Cl-CDs were typical spherical structure with good dispersity and uniformity in size. The size of N/Cl-CDs examined by high resolution TEM (HRTEM), the average diameter of N/Cl-CDs was found to be 3.46 nm and the spacing of the adjacent lattice planes was 0.24 nm.

The FT-IR spectrum was performed to determine the surface groups and state of synthesized N/Cl-CDs. It can be seen from Fig. 2d that the strong peaks at 3430, 3259 and 2950 cm−1 were corresponding to OH, NH and CH stretching vibrations. The absorption peak at 1649 cm−1 was assigned to the vibrations of CO and CC. The characteristic absorption peak at 1400 cm−1 was probably associated with the vibrations of CN bond. And the peak appeared around at 1089 cm−1 was put down to the stretching vibration of CO bond [2,33,34].

The surface functionalized groups of N/Cl-CDs were further characterized by the XPS spectra. The XPS spectrum of N/Cl-CDs showed three dominant peaks at 283.8, 398.3 and 531.0 eV, which were associated with C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively (Fig. 3a). The C 1s spectrum (Fig. 3b) could be divided into four peaks at 283.8, 284.7, 285.7, and 287.2 eV, which are associated with CC, CO, CN, and CO, respectively [13]. The N 1s spectrum in Fig. 3c has two typical peaks at 398.2 and 399.7 eV, attributed to NC and NH, respectively [35]. The high-resolution O 1s spectrum (Fig. 3d) could be decomposed into peaks at 530.3, 531.1, and 532.3 eV, indicating the presence of OH, CO, and CO, respectively [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence probe of N/Cl-CDs toward Hg2+

ce:section.ce:para: High selectivity is vital for an excellent fluorescent probe. We studied the fluorescence intensity change of N/Cl-CDs in the presence of different metal ions (Cr3+, Cr6+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Ag+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cd2+, Al3+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Co2+) and anions (SO4 2−, SO3 2−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, I−, Cl−, Br−, IO3 −, NO2 −, PO4 3−, S2−, BrO3 −, CN−) at the same conditions of 25 μM. It was noticeable that only Hg2+ can significantly quench the fluorescence intensity of N/Cl-CDs while other metal ions and anions display a slight effect on the fluorescence intensity of the N/Cl-CDs, which could be negligible (Fig. 4a and b). These results suggest that the N/Cl-CDs have a high sensitivity and selectivity to detect Hg2+.

The as-prepared N/Cl-CDs with quantum yield (37%) can be used to detect Hg2+, and the fluorescence intensity of N/Cl-CDs decreased in the presence of Hg2+. To study the sensitivity of N/Cl-CDs to Hg2+, different concentrations of Hg2+ were added into N/Cl-CDs solution and a few seconds of vortex treatment. The fluorescence intensities of N/Cl-CDs were measured after incubation for 5 min. As shown in Fig. 4c and d, introduction of Hg2+ caused the fluorescence intensities remarkably quenched, the fluorescence intensity of N/Cl-CDs gradually decreased with the increasing Hg2+ concentration. There was an excellent linear with standard deviation R2 = 0.9914 between fluorescent intensity of N/Cl-CDs and Hg2+ concentrations in the range of 0.2–40 μM (Fig. 4e). The associated linear regression equation was F/F0 = −0.021x + 0.964 (x represented the Hg2+ concentration: μM). The limit of detection (LOD) is calculated to be 0.05 μM based on 3σ/S (σ is the standard deviation of the response and S is the slope of the calibration curve) [37,38]. By comparison with the fluorescence methods for determination of Hg2+, linear range and detection limit of this work were comparable or even better than those reported literature (Table 1 ).

In order to deduce the possible mechanism of fluorescence quenching of the N/Cl-CDs by Hg2+, the UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence lifetime of this sensing system was recorded and compared. Fig. 5a shows that UV–vis spectra of N/Cl-CDs exhibited a strong absorption peak around 334 nm, but a significantly decreased change of the absorption peak after the addition of Hg2+ and it was blue-shifted to 323 nm. The Hg2+ may be have a strong affinity with the surface carboxyl, hydroxyl and amino group of N/Cl-CDs, and the complex chelate between Hg2+ and N/Cl-CDs is formed. In this case, the electron-transfer from the N/Cl-CDs to the Hg2+ was possibly resulted in the fluorescence quenching. The fluorescence lifetimes of samples were measured and the fluorescence decays were fit to bi-exponential decay kinetics (Fig. 5b). The average fluorescence lifetimes of N/Cl-CDs and N/Cl-CDs containing Hg2+ were 3.56 and 3.48 ns, respectively, which has no a significant change. Stern–Volmer curves for the N/Cl-CDs with the Hg2+ solution system can be analyzed at three different temperatures (298 K; 308 K and 318 K). It can be seen from Fig. 5c that the quenching constant decreases with the rise of temperature. The results indicated that the fluorescence quenching of N/Cl-CDs containing Hg2+ is contributed to static quenching.

To evaluate the feasibility of this method, river, pond, lake and tap water obtained as real samples and filtered with a 0.22 μm to remove insoluble particles before analysis. As shown in Table 2 , the addition of samples did not any significant changes to the fluorescence of N/Cl-CDs. Different concentrations of standard Hg2+ solutions were added into water sample, the recoveries obtained for the analyzed samples were calculated in the range of 96–108% with the RSD ranges were less than 6%. These results elucidated that the reliability of fluorescence probe of N/Cl-CDs for the detection of Hg2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antifungal effect of N/Cl-CDs on C. albicans
Photocatalytic disinfection tests to C. albicans
ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic antifungal property of N/Cl-CDs was evaluated by C. albicans. In the experiment, C. albicans, treated with the N/Cl-CDs of different concentrations, exposed to visible light illumination for different time intervals. Similarly, the C. albicans suspension was mixed with N/Cl-CDs solution under without light irradiation to test whether the N/Cl-CDs can inhibit the fungus in dark. As shown in Fig. 6a, C. albicans with N/Cl-CDs were treated in the light or in the dark for 60 min. The result indicated that there was a substantial photocatalytic antifungal property of N/Cl-CDs against C. albicans under visible light illumination, while a negligible inactivation on C. albicans in the dark. The optimum experimental concentration was 7 mg/mL. From Fig. 6b the antifungal efficiency was enhanced with the increase of visible light illumination time and N/Cl-CDs concentration only after the addition of N/Cl-CDs. In order to study the effect of N/Cl-CDs on C. albicans more clear, Fig. 6c displays only few C. albicans remain after 60 min, all of the C. albicans were killed after 80 min with the antifungal efficiency is upon 100% in the presence of N/Cl-CDs (7 mg/mL). The antifungal efficiency for 20 min visible light illumination time at different concentrations of N/Cl-CDs was shown in Fig. 6d. It showed that the antifungal efficiency increased with the increasing concentration of N/Cl-CDs, and N/Cl-CDs can kill C. albicans maintaining dependent on the concentration of N/Cl-CDs and visible light illumination time.

In order to interpret the damage process of fungal cells in N/Cl-CDs under visible light illumination, the morphological changes of C. albicans were observed by SEM at different time intervals. Fig. 7 showed fungus exhibited smooth and intact cell walls before photocatalytic antifungal inactivation. After 20 min of treatment, fungal surfaces started to wrinkle. And after 40 min of treatment, fungal surfaces wrinkled more serious and a few pits appeared on cell membrane. Subsequently, at the end of irradiation, the cell wall were deformed and distorted completely. These results indicated that C. albicans were completely killed by N/Cl-CDs under visible light irradiation.

According to the previous report, reactive oxygen species (ROS), such as hydroxyl radicals (HO•), superoxide ions (O2
−), hole (h+), damaged the cell membrane in the photocatalytic disinfection process [46,47]. As shown in Fig. 8 , e−-h+ pairs were produced within N/Cl-CDs under visible light illumination, electrons transferred effectively from conduction band (CB) to valance band (VB). The separated e− can react with O2 to generated O2
−, while HO can be generated from h+ reacting with adsorbed water or surface OH. The generated photogenerated e− and h+, as well as free radicals can damage the cell envelope integrity and lead them to dead.

The as-prepared N/Cl-CDs exhibited excellent fluorescence property, low toxicity and good biocompatibility, which can be used as fluorescence probe to label C. albicans. Due to their small size, the N/Cl-CDs were well up taken by C. albicans and the labeling of C. albicans by N/Cl-CDs was evaluated by CLSM. C. albicans exhibited an intense fluorescence after incubation with N/Cl-CDs for 1 h. As shown in Fig. 9 , multicolour fluorescence such as blue, green and red was observed when excited at different wavelengths of 405, 488 and 555 nm laser pulses, respectively. The analysis confirmed that N/Cl-CDs can label C. albicans in suspensions.

In summary, nitrogen and chloride co-doped CDs (N/Cl-CDs) were synthesized in a choline chloride−urea DESs with citric acid as the carbon source. The N/Cl-CDs exhibited excellent advantages such as high quantum yield and low toxicity, which can be used to detect Hg2+, inhibition of fungus and bioimaging. The fluorescence intensity of N/Cl-CDs can be selectively quenched by Hg2+, and the synthesized N/Cl-CDs were demonstrated to be a suitable fluorescent probe for Hg2+. The N/Cl-CDs showed a strong antifungal activity against C. albicans that the growth of fungus cells was inhibited under visible light illumination. Furthermore, N/Cl-CDs are successfully used as an effective fluorescent probe for multicolour fluorescence imaging to label C. albicans.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was strongly supported by the Analysis and Testing Foundation of Kunming University of Science and Technology (2018M20172118081).
